figures.caption: Figure 1: Two limitations of current IVIF quality assessment. (a) Misranking within the same scene: for a single source pair, traditional no-reference metrics (VIF, EN, SD, SF) rank the humandisfavoured fused image B above the human-preferred A (MOS µA=3.50 > µB=2.75). (b) One-hot MLLM-as-Judge lacks fidelity: two fused images with near-tie human MOS (3.75 vs. 3.50) are forced into distinct discrete levels (4 vs. 3) by an MLLM trained with one-hot supervision over five quality levels.
Table 1: Score regression on EVAFusion test (n=935, 85 source pairs). Bold: best per column. “—”: scalar-output methods. KL column: KL(ppred∥pGT) against the soft-Gaussian target of Eq. (6).

Table 5: Full score regression results on EVAFusion test (source-pair-disjoint split; n = 935 images, 85 source pairs). Bold marks the best in each column. “—” marks methods that emit a scalar quality estimate rather than a per-image quality distribution, so a comparable KL on the soft-Gaussianover-levels target is undefined for them. The KL column reports KL(ppred ∥ pGT) against the soft-Gaussian-over-levels target with σ from Eq. (5); for zero-shot baselines this is computed on each method’s emitted level distribution, while for both Ours rows it is computed by Gaussian-binning the predicted (µ̂, σ̂) back to the same five-level support.

Figure 3: Qualitative results on three representative source pairs (SP1–SP3). Top: fused images from 11 methods grouped into top/mid/bottom-quality tertiles by GT µ (annotated). Bottom: predicted scores from FuScore (red), DeQA-Score, Q-Align, and ViT-L+MLP across all 11 methods (sorted by GT µ, desc.), plotted against human GT (dashed). FuScore tracks the GT trajectory most closely, including the steep drop on bottom-tertile methods.
Figure 2: Framework of our FuScore. a) FuScore Inference for per image. b) Sub-dim-aware soft label construction. c) Tripartite training process.

Table 9: SRCC stratified by per-image dimensional conflict δi across 11 scoring methods on the EVAFusion test set (n = 935). δi is the standard deviation of the four sub-dimension scores per image (§3.2); higher δi indicates greater dimensional disagreement. Tertile boundaries chosen as described in App. B.3, yielding n = 468/218/249. Bold marks the best score per column across all 11 methods. † single-seed result; remaining ablation rows are 3-seed ensembles. ‡ diagnostic stack on top of our final composition.
